topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mahogany door clicked shut behind me, the sound echoing in the sterile quiet of Dr. Albright’s office. I clutched the strap of my worn messenger bag so tightly my knuckles were white. Across the desk sat Mark. Mark, who used to dismantle my lunch money and make sure I felt small enough to disappear. Now, he wore a crisp white coat, his demeanor radiating a calm, almost unnerving serenity. He offered a gentle, practiced smile. "Come in, Sarah. Have a seat." My legs felt suddenly weak. Every instinct screamed at me to bolt, to find the nearest exit, but my feet were rooted to the expensive carpet. I sank into the chair, my gaze fixed on a water stain on the ceiling, trying desperately to catalog every perceived flaw in his professional facade. "So," he began, his voice a low, soothing baritone, "tell me about what brought you in today." The silence stretched, thick and suffocating, filled only by the frantic drumming of my own pulse against my ribs.

[story 2]

I kept catching glimpses of him in the hallway, a silhouette against the muted lighting of the clinic corridor. It was him. Mark. He was reviewing files, his posture impeccable, the very picture of professional composure. My stomach twisted into a tight knot, a cold, nauseating sensation spreading through my chest. I tried to focus on the receptionist’s name tag, anything but the fact that the person who once made my life a constant source of low-grade terror was now the one guiding my anxieties. When he finally looked up, his eyes, the same sharp, assessing blue I remembered, met mine. There was no malice there, just a deep, almost sorrowful understanding. "It takes courage to seek help," he murmured, his tone devoid of judgment, yet somehow heavier than any harsh reprimand I’d ever received. I managed a jerky nod, my throat suddenly dry, unable to form a coherent response.

[story 3]

The air in the consultation room smelled faintly of lavender and old paper, a stark contrast to the stale scent of cheap cafeteria food I associated with Mark. I sat rigidly on the edge of the sofa, my hands folded so tightly in my lap that my fingers ached. He was leaning forward, resting his elbows on the desk, his expression one of profound, almost academic empathy. "We need to unpack the narrative you’ve built around that experience," he stated, his voice steady, methodical. Every word he spoke felt like a precise dissection of my most private failures. I swallowed hard, the movement catching in my throat. I wanted to apologize for existing, for having ever been the target of his scorn, but the words wouldn't surface. Instead, I just stared at the polished wood grain of the desk, wishing the floor would swallow me whole.
